Bayesian Analysis of the Normal Distribution

Prerequisites

This chapter builds on Bayesian Modeling. It develops conjugate Bayesian inference for the Gaussian distribution, which serves as a building block throughout the course.

Source
import torch
from torch.distributions import Gamma, TransformedDistribution, Normal, StudentT
from torch.distributions.transforms import PowerTransform
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from matplotlib.cm import Blues

Normal Model with Unknown Mean¶

Let’s start with the simplest nontrivial Bayesian model to get a feel for how the machinery works.

Example: Modeling SAT scores

Suppose we have SAT scores x1,…,xNx_1, \ldots, x_Nx1​,…,xN​ from NNN students in one class. We model the scores as conditionally independent Gaussians with unknown mean μ\muμ and known variance σ2\sigma^2σ2. Our goal is to infer μ\muμ from the data.

We place a Gaussian prior on μ\muμ and model each score as an i.i.d. draw from a Gaussian with that mean:

μ∼N(μ0,σ02),xn∣μ∼iidN(μ,σ2),n=1,…,N.\begin{align} \mu &\sim \cN(\mu_0, \sigma_0^2), \\ x_n \mid \mu &\iid{\sim} \cN(\mu, \sigma^2), \quad n = 1, \ldots, N. \end{align}μxn​∣μ​∼N(μ0​,σ02​),∼iidN(μ,σ2),n=1,…,N.​​
(1)

The hyperparameters are η=(μ0,σ02,σ2)\mbeta = (\mu_0, \sigma_0^2, \sigma^2)η=(μ0​,σ02​,σ2): the prior mean μ0\mu_0μ0​, prior variance σ02\sigma_0^2σ02​, and (known) data variance σ2\sigma^2σ2. The prior variance σ02\sigma_0^2σ02​ encodes how uncertain we are about the mean before seeing any data — a large σ02\sigma_0^2σ02​ corresponds to a vague, spread-out prior.

Computing the Posterior¶

By Bayes’ rule, the posterior is proportional to the prior times the likelihood. Expanding the Gaussian densities,

p(μ∣X;η)∝p(μ;η)∏n=1Np(xn∣μ;η)=N(μ;μ0,σ02)∏n=1NN(xn;μ,σ2)∝exp⁡{−(μ−μ0)22σ02}∏n=1Nexp⁡{−(xn−μ)22σ2}.\begin{align} p(\mu \mid \mbX; \mbeta) &\propto p(\mu; \mbeta) \prod_{n=1}^N p(x_n \mid \mu; \mbeta) \\ &= \cN(\mu; \mu_0, \sigma_0^2) \prod_{n=1}^N \cN(x_n; \mu, \sigma^2) \\ &\propto \exp \left\{ -\frac{(\mu - \mu_0)^2}{2 \sigma_0^2} \right\} \prod_{n=1}^N \exp \left\{ -\frac{(x_n - \mu)^2}{2 \sigma^2} \right\}. \end{align}p(μ∣X;η)​∝p(μ;η)n=1∏N​p(xn​∣μ;η)=N(μ;μ0​,σ02​)n=1∏N​N(xn​;μ,σ2)∝exp{−2σ02​(μ−μ0​)2​}n=1∏N​exp{−2σ2(xn​−μ)2​}.​​
(2)

Since all factors are exponentials of quadratics in μ\muμ, the product is also an exponential of a quadratic in μ\muμ — which means the posterior is Gaussian. This is what it means for the Gaussian prior to be conjugate to the Gaussian likelihood.

To identify the posterior, we collect the terms quadratic and linear in μ\muμ in the exponent:

p(μ∣X;η)∝exp⁡{−12JNμ2+hNμ},\begin{align} p(\mu \mid \mbX; \mbeta) &\propto \exp \left\{ -\frac{1}{2} J_N \mu^2 + h_N \mu \right\}, \end{align}p(μ∣X;η)​∝exp{−21​JN​μ2+hN​μ},​​
(3)

where we define the posterior precision and posterior information,

JN=1σ02+Nσ2,hN=μ0σ02+∑n=1Nxnσ2.\begin{align} J_N = \frac{1}{\sigma_0^2} + \frac{N}{\sigma^2}, \qquad h_N = \frac{\mu_0}{\sigma_0^2} + \sum_{n=1}^N \frac{x_n}{\sigma^2}. \end{align}JN​=σ02​1​+σ2N​,hN​=σ02​μ0​​+n=1∑N​σ2xn​​.​​
(4)

The precision JNJ_NJN​ is simply the sum of the prior precision 1/σ021/\sigma_0^21/σ02​ and the total data precision N/σ2N/\sigma^2N/σ2 — information is additive. The information vector hNh_NhN​ similarly sums contributions from the prior and each data point.

Completing the Square¶

The exponent −12JNμ2+hNμ-\frac{1}{2} J_N \mu^2 + h_N \mu−21​JN​μ2+hN​μ is a quadratic in μ\muμ. We can complete the square to convert it to the form −12σN2(μ−μN)2+const-\frac{1}{2\sigma_N^2}(\mu - \mu_N)^2 + \text{const}−2σN2​1​(μ−μN​)2+const, where const\text{const}const does not depend on μ\muμ. Specifically:

−12JNμ2+hNμ=−JN2(μ2−2hNJNμ)=−JN2(μ−hNJN)2+hN22JN.\begin{align} -\frac{1}{2} J_N \mu^2 + h_N \mu &= -\frac{J_N}{2}\left(\mu^2 - \frac{2 h_N}{J_N} \mu \right) = -\frac{J_N}{2}\left(\mu - \frac{h_N}{J_N}\right)^2 + \frac{h_N^2}{2 J_N}. \end{align}−21​JN​μ2+hN​μ​=−2JN​​(μ2−JN​2hN​​μ)=−2JN​​(μ−JN​hN​​)2+2JN​hN2​​.​​
(5)

Dropping the constant and recognizing σN2=JN−1\sigma_N^2 = J_N^{-1}σN2​=JN−1​ and μN=JN−1hN=hN/JN\mu_N = J_N^{-1} h_N = h_N / J_NμN​=JN−1​hN​=hN​/JN​, we find

p(μ∣X;η)=N(μ∣μN,σN2),\begin{align} p(\mu \mid \mbX; \mbeta) &= \cN(\mu \mid \mu_N, \sigma_N^2), \end{align}p(μ∣X;η)​=N(μ∣μN​,σN2​),​​
(6)

where

σN2=(1σ02+Nσ2)−1,μN=σN2(μ0σ02+Nxˉσ2)=σ2σ2+Nσ02 μ0+Nσ02σ2+Nσ02 μML,\begin{align} \sigma_N^2 &= \left(\frac{1}{\sigma_0^2} + \frac{N}{\sigma^2}\right)^{-1}, \\[4pt] \mu_N &= \sigma_N^2 \left(\frac{\mu_0}{\sigma_0^2} + \frac{N \bar{x}}{\sigma^2}\right) = \frac{\sigma^2}{\sigma^2 + N\sigma_0^2}\, \mu_0 + \frac{N\sigma_0^2}{\sigma^2 + N\sigma_0^2}\, \mu_{\mathsf{ML}}, \end{align}σN2​μN​​=(σ02​1​+σ2N​)−1,=σN2​(σ02​μ0​​+σ2Nxˉ​)=σ2+Nσ02​σ2​μ0​+σ2+Nσ02​Nσ02​​μML​,​​
(7)

with xˉ=1N∑n=1Nxn=μML\bar{x} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=1}^N x_n = \mu_{\mathsf{ML}}xˉ=N1​∑n=1N​xn​=μML​ being the sample mean (and maximum likelihood estimate).

Interpreting the Posterior¶

The posterior mean μN\mu_NμN​ is a weighted average of the prior mean μ0\mu_0μ0​ and the MLE μML\mu_{\mathsf{ML}}μML​. The weights are determined by the relative precision of the prior versus the data. When NNN is large, almost all weight goes to the data and μN→μML\mu_N \to \mu_{\mathsf{ML}}μN​→μML​. When the prior is diffuse (σ02→∞\sigma_0^2 \to \inftyσ02​→∞, i.e., an uninformative prior), the prior precision vanishes and again μN→μML\mu_N \to \mu_{\mathsf{ML}}μN​→μML​.

The posterior variance σN2\sigma_N^2σN2​ follows a simpler pattern via the precision:

1σN2=1σ02+Nσ2.\begin{align} \frac{1}{\sigma_N^2} &= \frac{1}{\sigma_0^2} + \frac{N}{\sigma^2}. \end{align}σN2​1​​=σ02​1​+σ2N​.​​
(8)

Each new observation contributes 1/σ21/\sigma^21/σ2 of additional precision. As N→∞N \to \inftyN→∞, the posterior variance shrinks to zero — we become increasingly certain about μ\muμ. Under an uninformative prior (σ02→∞\sigma_0^2 \to \inftyσ02​→∞), the posterior variance becomes σN2=σ2/N\sigma_N^2 = \sigma^2/NσN2​=σ2/N, the familiar sampling variance of the mean.

Source
torch.manual_seed(305)

# Model hyperparameters and true parameter
μ0    = torch.tensor(0.0)
σ2_0  = torch.tensor(1.0)
σ2    = torch.tensor(1.0)

# Simulate data
N  = 20
μ  = Normal(μ0, torch.sqrt(σ2_0)).sample()
X  = Normal(μ, torch.sqrt(σ2)).expand([N]).sample()

# Compute posterior parameters
J_N     = 1 / σ2_0 + N / σ2
h_N     = μ0 / σ2_0 + X.sum() / σ2
μ_N     = h_N / J_N
σ2_N    = 1 / J_N

# Plot prior, likelihood, and posterior
prior = Normal(μ0, torch.sqrt(σ2_0))
lkhd  = Normal(μ, torch.sqrt(σ2))
post  = Normal(μ_N, torch.sqrt(σ2_N))
grid  = torch.linspace(-5, 5, 500)

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot(grid, torch.exp(prior.log_prob(grid)),
        ':r', label=r"$p(\mu \mid \mu_0, \sigma_0^2)$")
ax.plot(grid, torch.exp(lkhd.log_prob(grid)),
        '-b', label=r"$p(x \mid \mu, \sigma^2)$")
ax.plot(grid, torch.exp(post.log_prob(grid)),
        '-', color='purple', label=r"$p(\mu \mid X, \mu_0, \sigma_0^2, \sigma^2)$")
ax.axvline(μ.item(), color='r', linestyle='--', lw=1, label=r"true $\mu$")
for n, x in enumerate(X):
    ax.axvline(x.item(), color='k', lw=0.5, alpha=0.4,
               label=r"$x_n$" if n == 0 else None)
ax.set_xlabel(r"$\mu$")
ax.set_ylabel("density")
ax.set_xlim(-5, 5)
ax.legend(fontsize=11)
ax.set_title("Prior, likelihood, and posterior for the Gaussian mean")
plt.tight_layout()

Normal Model with Unknown Precision¶

Now suppose the mean μ\muμ is known but the variance is not. Our calculations are slightly cleaner if we work with the precision λ=1/σ2\lambda = 1/\sigma^2λ=1/σ2 rather than the variance directly. In terms of the precision, the Gaussian density is,

p(x∣μ,λ)=(λ2π)12exp⁡{−λ2(x−μ)2}.\begin{align} p(x \mid \mu, \lambda) &= \left(\frac{\lambda}{2\pi}\right)^{\frac{1}{2}} \exp\left\{-\frac{\lambda}{2}(x - \mu)^2\right\}. \end{align}p(x∣μ,λ)​=(2πλ​)21​exp{−2λ​(x−μ)2}.​​
(9)

What prior should we place on λ\lambdaλ? We need a distribution on R+\reals_+R+​ (since λ>0\lambda > 0λ>0), ideally one that is conjugate to the Gaussian likelihood. The chi-squared distribution fits the bill naturally.

The Chi-Squared Distribution¶

Let z1,…,zν∼iidN(0,1)z_1, \ldots, z_\nu \iid{\sim} \cN(0, 1)z1​,…,zν​∼iidN(0,1) and define λ=∑i=1νzi2\lambda = \sum_{i=1}^\nu z_i^2λ=∑i=1ν​zi2​. Then λ\lambdaλ follows a chi-squared distribution with ν\nuν degrees of freedom,

λ∼χ2(ν),\begin{align} \lambda \sim \chi^2(\nu), \end{align}λ∼χ2(ν),​​
(10)

with pdf

χ2(λ;ν)=12ν/2Γ(ν/2)λν/2−1e−λ/2.\begin{align} \chi^2(\lambda; \nu) &= \frac{1}{2^{\nu/2} \Gamma(\nu/2)} \lambda^{\nu/2 - 1} e^{-\lambda/2}. \end{align}χ2(λ;ν)​=2ν/2Γ(ν/2)1​λν/2−1e−λ/2.​​
(11)

This is a special case of the gamma distribution: χ2(ν)=Ga(ν/2, 1/2)\chi^2(\nu) = \mathrm{Ga}(\nu/2,\, 1/2)χ2(ν)=Ga(ν/2,1/2) using the rate parameterization. Its mean is E[λ]=ν\E[\lambda] = \nuE[λ]=ν and its variance is Var[λ]=2ν\Var[\lambda] = 2\nuVar[λ]=2ν.

The Scaled Chi-Squared Distribution¶

The standard chi-squared has mean ν\nuν, which grows with the degrees of freedom. For a prior over precision, it is more convenient to control the mean independently of the degrees of freedom. We do this by drawing from a scaled version: let zi∼iidN(0,λ0)z_i \iid{\sim} \cN(0, \lambda_0)zi​∼iidN(0,λ0​) and define λ=1ν0∑i=1ν0zi2\lambda = \frac{1}{\nu_0} \sum_{i=1}^{\nu_0} z_i^2λ=ν0​1​∑i=1ν0​​zi2​ (note the average rather than the sum). Then we say

λ∼χ2(ν0,λ0),\begin{align} \lambda \sim \chi^2(\nu_0, \lambda_0), \end{align}λ∼χ2(ν0​,λ0​),​​
(12)

where λ0>0\lambda_0 > 0λ0​>0 is the scale parameter. Since zi=λ0z~iz_i = \sqrt{\lambda_0} \tilde{z}_izi​=λ0​​z~i​ with z~i∼iidN(0,1)\tilde{z}_i \iid{\sim} \cN(0,1)z~i​∼iidN(0,1), we have λ=λ0ν0∑i=1ν0z~i2\lambda = \frac{\lambda_0}{\nu_0} \sum_{i=1}^{\nu_0} \tilde{z}_i^2λ=ν0​λ0​​∑i=1ν0​​z~i2​, so λ=λ0ν0λ~\lambda = \frac{\lambda_0}{\nu_0} \tilde{\lambda}λ=ν0​λ0​​λ~ where λ~∼χ2(ν0)\tilde{\lambda} \sim \chi^2(\nu_0)λ~∼χ2(ν0​). Using the fact that the gamma distribution is closed under multiplicative scaling, λ∼Ga(ν0/2, ν0/(2λ0))\lambda \sim \mathrm{Ga}(\nu_0/2,\, \nu_0 / (2\lambda_0))λ∼Ga(ν0​/2,ν0​/(2λ0​)), giving the pdf

χ2(λ;ν0,λ0)=(ν02λ0)ν0/2Γ(ν0/2) λν0/2−1exp⁡ ⁣(−ν0λ2λ0).\begin{align} \chi^2(\lambda; \nu_0, \lambda_0) &= \frac{\left(\frac{\nu_0}{2\lambda_0}\right)^{\nu_0/2}}{\Gamma(\nu_0/2)}\, \lambda^{\nu_0/2 - 1} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\nu_0 \lambda}{2\lambda_0}\right). \end{align}χ2(λ;ν0​,λ0​)​=Γ(ν0​/2)(2λ0​ν0​​)ν0​/2​λν0​/2−1exp(−2λ0​ν0​λ​).​​
(13)

The mean is E[λ]=λ0\E[\lambda] = \lambda_0E[λ]=λ0​ regardless of ν0\nu_0ν0​, and the variance is Var[λ]=2λ02/ν0\Var[\lambda] = 2\lambda_0^2 / \nu_0Var[λ]=2λ02​/ν0​. The parameter ν0\nu_0ν0​ controls concentration: larger ν0\nu_0ν0​ gives a tighter distribution around its mean λ0\lambda_0λ0​.

Source
class ScaledChiSq(Gamma):
    """Scaled chi-squared distribution χ²(ν, λ).

    Equivalent to Ga(ν/2, ν/(2λ)) using the rate parameterisation.
    The mean is λ and the variance is 2λ²/ν.
    """
    def __init__(self, ν, λ):
        Gamma.__init__(self, ν / 2, ν / (2 * λ))
        self.ν = ν
        self.λ = λ


λ0   = torch.tensor(2.)
grid = torch.linspace(1e-3, 10, 200)
dofs = [2, 4, 6, 8, 10]

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
for i, ν in enumerate(dofs):
    chisq = ScaledChiSq(torch.tensor(float(ν)), λ0)
    ax.plot(grid, torch.exp(chisq.log_prob(grid)),
            label=r"$\chi^2({}, {})$".format(ν, int(λ0)),
            color=Blues(0.3 + 0.7 * i / (len(dofs) - 1)))

ax.legend(fontsize=12)
ax.set_xlabel(r"$\lambda$")
ax.set_ylabel(r"$p(\lambda)$")
ax.set_title(r"Scaled $\chi^2$ Distribution ($\lambda_0=2$, varying $\nu_0$)")
plt.tight_layout()

Conjugate Update¶

With the scaled chi-squared prior, the model is

λ∼χ2(ν0,λ0),xn∣μ,λ∼iidN(μ,1/λ),n=1,…,N.\begin{align} \lambda &\sim \chi^2(\nu_0, \lambda_0), \\ x_n \mid \mu, \lambda &\iid{\sim} \cN(\mu, 1/\lambda), \quad n = 1, \ldots, N. \end{align}λxn​∣μ,λ​∼χ2(ν0​,λ0​),∼iidN(μ,1/λ),n=1,…,N.​​
(14)

The chi-squared distribution is conjugate to the Gaussian likelihood in λ\lambdaλ. Letting η=(μ,ν0,λ0)\mbeta = (\mu, \nu_0, \lambda_0)η=(μ,ν0​,λ0​), we compute:

p(λ∣X;η)∝χ2(λ;ν0,λ0)∏n=1NN(xn;μ,1λ)∝λν0/2−1e−ν0λ/(2λ0)⋅∏n=1Nλ1/2e−λ2(xn−μ)2∝λ(ν0+N)/2−1exp⁡ ⁣(−λ2[ν0λ0+∑n=1N(xn−μ)2]).\begin{align} p(\lambda \mid \mbX; \mbeta) &\propto \chi^2(\lambda; \nu_0, \lambda_0) \prod_{n=1}^N \cN(x_n; \mu, \tfrac{1}{\lambda}) \\ &\propto \lambda^{\nu_0/2 - 1} e^{-\nu_0 \lambda / (2\lambda_0)} \cdot \prod_{n=1}^N \lambda^{1/2} e^{-\frac{\lambda}{2}(x_n - \mu)^2} \\ &\propto \lambda^{(\nu_0 + N)/2 - 1} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\lambda}{2}\left[\frac{\nu_0}{\lambda_0} + \sum_{n=1}^N (x_n - \mu)^2\right]\right). \end{align}p(λ∣X;η)​∝χ2(λ;ν0​,λ0​)n=1∏N​N(xn​;μ,λ1​)∝λν0​/2−1e−ν0​λ/(2λ0​)⋅n=1∏N​λ1/2e−2λ​(xn​−μ)2∝λ(ν0​+N)/2−1exp(−2λ​[λ0​ν0​​+n=1∑N​(xn​−μ)2]).​​
(15)

This is another scaled chi-squared distribution, p(λ∣X;η)=χ2(λ;νN,λN)p(\lambda \mid \mbX; \mbeta) = \chi^2(\lambda; \nu_N, \lambda_N)p(λ∣X;η)=χ2(λ;νN​,λN​), where

νN=ν0+N,λN=νN(ν0λ0+∑n=1N(xn−μ)2)−1.\begin{align} \nu_N &= \nu_0 + N, \\ \lambda_N &= \nu_N \left(\frac{\nu_0}{\lambda_0} + \sum_{n=1}^N (x_n - \mu)^2\right)^{-1}. \end{align}νN​λN​​=ν0​+N,=νN​(λ0​ν0​​+n=1∑N​(xn​−μ)2)−1.​​
(16)

The posterior degrees of freedom grow by one per observation. As ν0→0\nu_0 \to 0ν0​→0 (an uninformative prior), the posterior mean converges to λN→1/(1N∑n(xn−μ)2)\lambda_N \to 1/\left(\frac{1}{N}\sum_n (x_n - \mu)^2\right)λN​→1/(N1​∑n​(xn​−μ)2), the inverse of the sample variance — as expected.

Normal Model with Unknown Variance¶

Working with the precision is mathematically natural, but it can be more intuitive to parameterize the model in terms of the variance σ2=1/λ\sigma^2 = 1/\lambdaσ2=1/λ. If λ∼χ2(ν0,λ0)\lambda \sim \chi^2(\nu_0, \lambda_0)λ∼χ2(ν0​,λ0​), then the change-of-variables formula gives the distribution of σ2=1/λ\sigma^2 = 1/\lambdaσ2=1/λ:

p(σ2∣ν0,σ02)=∣ ⁣d(1/σ2) ⁣dσ2∣χ2 ⁣(1σ2;ν0,1σ02)=1(σ2)2⋅(ν02σ0−2)ν0/2Γ(ν0/2)(1σ2)ν0/2−1exp⁡ ⁣(−ν02σ02σ2)=(ν0σ022)ν0/2Γ(ν0/2) (σ2)−ν0/2−1exp⁡ ⁣(−ν0σ022σ2)≜χ−2(σ2∣ν0,σ02),\begin{align} p(\sigma^2 \mid \nu_0, \sigma_0^2) &= \left|\frac{\dif (1/\sigma^2)}{\dif \sigma^2}\right| \chi^2\!\left(\frac{1}{\sigma^2}; \nu_0, \frac{1}{\sigma_0^2}\right) \\ &= \frac{1}{(\sigma^2)^2} \cdot \frac{\left(\frac{\nu_0}{2\sigma_0^{-2}}\right)^{\nu_0/2}}{\Gamma(\nu_0/2)} \left(\frac{1}{\sigma^2}\right)^{\nu_0/2 - 1} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\nu_0}{2\sigma_0^2 \sigma^2}\right) \\ &= \frac{\left(\frac{\nu_0 \sigma_0^2}{2}\right)^{\nu_0/2}}{\Gamma(\nu_0/2)}\, (\sigma^2)^{-\nu_0/2 - 1} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\nu_0 \sigma_0^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) \\ &\triangleq \chi^{-2}(\sigma^2 \mid \nu_0, \sigma_0^2), \end{align}p(σ2∣ν0​,σ02​)​=∣∣​dσ2d(1/σ2)​∣∣​χ2(σ21​;ν0​,σ02​1​)=(σ2)21​⋅Γ(ν0​/2)(2σ0−2​ν0​​)ν0​/2​(σ21​)ν0​/2−1exp(−2σ02​σ2ν0​​)=Γ(ν0​/2)(2ν0​σ02​​)ν0​/2​(σ2)−ν0​/2−1exp(−2σ2ν0​σ02​​)≜χ−2(σ2∣ν0​,σ02​),​​
(17)

where σ02=1/λ0\sigma_0^2 = 1/\lambda_0σ02​=1/λ0​. This is the scaled inverse chi-squared distribution. It is a special case of the inverse gamma: χ−2(ν0,σ02)=IGa(ν0/2, ν0σ02/2)\chi^{-2}(\nu_0, \sigma_0^2) = \mathrm{IGa}(\nu_0/2,\, \nu_0 \sigma_0^2 / 2)χ−2(ν0​,σ02​)=IGa(ν0​/2,ν0​σ02​/2).

The mean is E[σ2]=ν0ν0−2σ02\E[\sigma^2] = \frac{\nu_0}{\nu_0 - 2} \sigma_0^2E[σ2]=ν0​−2ν0​​σ02​ (for ν0>2\nu_0 > 2ν0​>2) and the mode is ν0ν0+2σ02\frac{\nu_0}{\nu_0 + 2} \sigma_0^2ν0​+2ν0​​σ02​. The parameter σ02\sigma_0^2σ02​ is the prior “best guess” for the variance and ν0\nu_0ν0​ controls how strongly that guess is held.

Source
class ScaledInvChiSq(TransformedDistribution):
    """Scaled inverse chi-squared: χ⁻²(ν, σ²).

    Equivalent to IGa(ν/2, νσ²/2) using the rate parameterisation.
    The mean is σ²·ν/(ν-2) and the mode is σ²·ν/(ν+2).
    """
    def __init__(self, ν, σ2):
        base = Gamma(ν / 2, ν * σ2 / 2)
        TransformedDistribution.__init__(self, base, [PowerTransform(-1)])
        self.ν  = ν
        self.σ2 = σ2


σ2_0 = torch.tensor(2.)
grid  = torch.linspace(1e-3, 10, 200)
dofs  = [2, 4, 6, 8, 10]

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
for i, ν in enumerate(dofs):
    inv_chisq = ScaledInvChiSq(torch.tensor(float(ν)), σ2_0)
    ax.plot(grid, torch.exp(inv_chisq.log_prob(grid)),
            label=r"$\chi^{{-2}}({}, {})$".format(ν, int(σ2_0)),
            color=Blues(0.3 + 0.7 * i / (len(dofs) - 1)))

ax.legend(fontsize=12)
ax.set_xlabel(r"$\sigma^2$")
ax.set_ylabel(r"$p(\sigma^2)$")
ax.set_title(r"Scaled $\chi^{{-2}}$ Distribution ($\sigma_0^2=2$, varying $\nu_0$)")
plt.tight_layout()

Parameterized in terms of the variance, the model is

σ2∼χ−2(ν0,σ02),xn∣μ,σ2∼iidN(μ,σ2).\begin{align} \sigma^2 &\sim \chi^{-2}(\nu_0, \sigma_0^2), \\ x_n \mid \mu, \sigma^2 &\iid{\sim} \cN(\mu, \sigma^2). \end{align}σ2xn​∣μ,σ2​∼χ−2(ν0​,σ02​),∼iidN(μ,σ2).​​
(18)
Exercise

Show that the posterior is p(σ2∣X;η)=χ−2(σ2;νN,σN2)p(\sigma^2 \mid \mbX; \mbeta) = \chi^{-2}(\sigma^2; \nu_N, \sigma_N^2)p(σ2∣X;η)=χ−2(σ2;νN​,σN2​) where

νN=ν0+N,σN2=1νN(ν0σ02+∑n=1N(xn−μ)2).\begin{align} \nu_N &= \nu_0 + N, \\ \sigma_N^2 &= \frac{1}{\nu_N}\left(\nu_0 \sigma_0^2 + \sum_{n=1}^N (x_n - \mu)^2\right). \end{align}νN​σN2​​=ν0​+N,=νN​1​(ν0​σ02​+n=1∑N​(xn​−μ)2).​​
(19)

The posterior scale σN2\sigma_N^2σN2​ is the average of the prior sum of squares ν0σ02\nu_0 \sigma_0^2ν0​σ02​ and the data sum of squares ∑n(xn−μ)2\sum_n (x_n - \mu)^2∑n​(xn​−μ)2, weighted by ν0\nu_0ν0​ and NNN respectively. As ν0→0\nu_0 \to 0ν0​→0, σN2\sigma_N^2σN2​ converges to the sample variance.

Normal Model with Unknown Mean and Variance¶

Finally, suppose both μ\muμ and σ2\sigma^2σ2 are unknown. We want a joint conjugate prior over (μ,σ2)(\mu, \sigma^2)(μ,σ2). The key insight is to write the prior as a product p(μ,σ2)=p(μ∣σ2) p(σ2)p(\mu, \sigma^2) = p(\mu \mid \sigma^2) \, p(\sigma^2)p(μ,σ2)=p(μ∣σ2)p(σ2): an inverse chi-squared prior on the variance, and conditional on the variance, a Gaussian prior on the mean whose variance scales with σ2\sigma^2σ2. Specifically,

p(μ,σ2)=N(μ;μ0,σ2/κ0) χ−2(σ2;ν0,σ02)≜NIX(μ,σ2;μ0,κ0,ν0,σ02).\begin{align} p(\mu, \sigma^2) &= \cN(\mu; \mu_0, \sigma^2 / \kappa_0) \, \chi^{-2}(\sigma^2; \nu_0, \sigma_0^2) \\ &\triangleq \mathrm{NIX}(\mu, \sigma^2; \mu_0, \kappa_0, \nu_0, \sigma_0^2). \end{align}p(μ,σ2)​=N(μ;μ0​,σ2/κ0​)χ−2(σ2;ν0​,σ02​)≜NIX(μ,σ2;μ0​,κ0​,ν0​,σ02​).​​
(20)

This is the normal-inverse-chi-squared (NIX) distribution — our first truly bivariate distribution. It has four hyperparameters: μ0\mu_0μ0​ (prior mean), κ0\kappa_0κ0​ (mean confidence, in pseudo-observations), ν0\nu_0ν0​ (variance degrees of freedom), and σ02\sigma_0^2σ02​ (prior variance scale).

The coupling p(μ∣σ2)=N(μ0,σ2/κ0)p(\mu \mid \sigma^2) = \cN(\mu_0, \sigma^2/\kappa_0)p(μ∣σ2)=N(μ0​,σ2/κ0​) is crucial: the uncertainty in the mean is proportional to the variance. When σ2\sigma^2σ2 is large (we are uncertain about individual observations), we are also more uncertain about the mean. This dependence structure is what makes the NIX prior jointly conjugate.

Source
torch.manual_seed(305)

μ0   = torch.tensor(0.)
κ0   = torch.tensor(1.0)
ν0   = torch.tensor(2.0)
σ2_0 = torch.tensor(2.0)

def sample_nix(μ0, κ0, ν0, σ2_0, n):
    σ2 = ScaledInvChiSq(ν0, σ2_0).sample(sample_shape=(n,))
    μ  = Normal(μ0, torch.sqrt(σ2 / κ0)).sample()
    return μ, σ2

# Evaluate the joint density on a grid
μ_grid, σ2_grid = torch.meshgrid(
    torch.linspace(-5, 5, 60),
    torch.linspace(1e-3, 10, 60),
    indexing='ij',
)
log_prob  = ScaledInvChiSq(ν0, σ2_0).log_prob(σ2_grid)
log_prob += Normal(μ0, torch.sqrt(σ2_grid / κ0)).log_prob(μ_grid)

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
cf = ax.contourf(μ_grid, σ2_grid, torch.exp(log_prob), 25)
μ_samp, σ2_samp = sample_nix(μ0, κ0, ν0, σ2_0, 25)
ax.scatter(μ_samp.numpy(), σ2_samp.numpy(),
           c='w', edgecolors='k', s=40, zorder=5)
ax.set_xlim(-5, 5)
ax.set_ylim(0, 10)
ax.set_xlabel(r"$\mu$")
ax.set_ylabel(r"$\sigma^2$")
ax.set_title(r"$\mathrm{{NIX}}(\mu_0={:.0f},\,\kappa_0={:.0f},\,\nu_0={:.0f},\,\sigma_0^2={:.0f})$".format(
    μ0, κ0, ν0, σ2_0))
plt.colorbar(cf, ax=ax, label=r"$p(\mu, \sigma^2)$")
plt.tight_layout()

Posterior Update¶

With i.i.d. Gaussian observations xn∼iidN(μ,σ2)x_n \iid{\sim} \cN(\mu, \sigma^2)xn​∼iidN(μ,σ2), the posterior under the NIX prior is again NIX:

Exercise

Show that p(μ,σ2∣X;η)=NIX(μN,κN,νN,σN2)p(\mu, \sigma^2 \mid \mbX; \mbeta) = \mathrm{NIX}(\mu_N, \kappa_N, \nu_N, \sigma_N^2)p(μ,σ2∣X;η)=NIX(μN​,κN​,νN​,σN2​), where

κN=κ0+N,νN=ν0+N,μN=κ0μ0+∑n=1NxnκN=κ0κNμ0+NκNμML,σN2=1νN(ν0σ02+κ0μ02+∑n=1Nxn2−κNμN2).\begin{align} \kappa_N &= \kappa_0 + N, \\ \nu_N &= \nu_0 + N, \\ \mu_N &= \frac{\kappa_0 \mu_0 + \sum_{n=1}^N x_n}{\kappa_N} = \frac{\kappa_0}{\kappa_N}\mu_0 + \frac{N}{\kappa_N}\mu_{\mathsf{ML}}, \\ \sigma_N^2 &= \frac{1}{\nu_N}\left(\nu_0 \sigma_0^2 + \kappa_0 \mu_0^2 + \sum_{n=1}^N x_n^2 - \kappa_N \mu_N^2\right). \end{align}κN​νN​μN​σN2​​=κ0​+N,=ν0​+N,=κN​κ0​μ0​+∑n=1N​xn​​=κN​κ0​​μ0​+κN​N​μML​,=νN​1​(ν0​σ02​+κ0​μ02​+n=1∑N​xn2​−κN​μN2​).​​
(21)

Hint: write the prior and likelihood as exponentials of quadratics in (μ,1/σ2)(\mu, 1/\sigma^2)(μ,1/σ2) and collect terms.

Just as in the known-variance case, μN\mu_NμN​ is a weighted average of the prior mean and the sample mean. The posterior variance scale σN2\sigma_N^2σN2​ can be rewritten as σN2=1νN(ν0σ02+κ0μ02+∑nxn2−κNμN2)\sigma_N^2 = \frac{1}{\nu_N}\left(\nu_0 \sigma_0^2 + \kappa_0 \mu_0^2 + \sum_n x_n^2 - \kappa_N \mu_N^2\right)σN2​=νN​1​(ν0​σ02​+κ0​μ02​+∑n​xn2​−κN​μN2​), which combines the prior sum of squares with the data sum of squares, adjusted for the updated mean.

In the uninformative limit ν0→0\nu_0 \to 0ν0​→0, κ0→0\kappa_0 \to 0κ0​→0, the posterior parameters become νN=N\nu_N = NνN​=N, κN=N\kappa_N = NκN​=N, μN=μML\mu_N = \mu_{\mathsf{ML}}μN​=μML​, and σN2=σML2=1N∑n(xn−μML)2\sigma_N^2 = \sigma^2_{\mathsf{ML}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_n (x_n - \mu_{\mathsf{ML}})^2σN2​=σML2​=N1​∑n​(xn​−μML​)2.

Posterior Marginals: Student’s t Distribution¶

What are the marginal distributions of μ\muμ and σ2\sigma^2σ2 under the NIX posterior? The marginal of σ2\sigma^2σ2 is straightforward — since p(μ,σ2∣X)p(\mu, \sigma^2 \mid \mbX)p(μ,σ2∣X) is NIX, its σ2\sigma^2σ2 marginal is χ−2(νN,σN2)\chi^{-2}(\nu_N, \sigma_N^2)χ−2(νN​,σN2​).

The marginal of μ\muμ is more interesting. We integrate out σ2\sigma^2σ2:

p(μ∣X;η)=∫p(μ,σ2∣X;η) ⁣dσ2=∫N(μ;μN,σ2/κN) χ−2(σ2;νN,σN2) ⁣dσ2=St ⁣(μ; νN,μN,σN2/κN),\begin{align} p(\mu \mid \mbX; \mbeta) &= \int p(\mu, \sigma^2 \mid \mbX; \mbeta) \dif \sigma^2 \\ &= \int \cN(\mu; \mu_N, \sigma^2 / \kappa_N) \, \chi^{-2}(\sigma^2; \nu_N, \sigma_N^2) \dif \sigma^2 \\ &= \mathrm{St}\!\left(\mu;\, \nu_N, \mu_N, \sigma_N^2 / \kappa_N\right), \end{align}p(μ∣X;η)​=∫p(μ,σ2∣X;η)dσ2=∫N(μ;μN​,σ2/κN​)χ−2(σ2;νN​,σN2​)dσ2=St(μ;νN​,μN​,σN2​/κN​),​​
(22)

where St(ν,μ,σ2)\mathrm{St}(\nu, \mu, \sigma^2)St(ν,μ,σ2) denotes the Student’s t distribution with ν\nuν degrees of freedom, location μ\muμ, and scale σ2\sigma^2σ2, with density

St(x;ν,μ,σ2)=Γ ⁣(ν+12)Γ ⁣(ν2)1πνσ2[1+(x−μ)2νσ2]−ν+12.\begin{align} \mathrm{St}(x; \nu, \mu, \sigma^2) &= \frac{\Gamma\!\left(\frac{\nu+1}{2}\right)}{\Gamma\!\left(\frac{\nu}{2}\right)} \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi \nu \sigma^2}} \left[1 + \frac{(x - \mu)^2}{\nu \sigma^2}\right]^{-\frac{\nu+1}{2}}. \end{align}St(x;ν,μ,σ2)​=Γ(2ν​)Γ(2ν+1​)​πνσ2​1​[1+νσ2(x−μ)2​]−2ν+1​.​​
(23)

Its mean is μ\muμ and its variance is νν−2σ2\frac{\nu}{\nu - 2}\sigma^2ν−2ν​σ2 for ν>2\nu > 2ν>2.

The Student’s t arises here because we are averaging a Gaussian over a random variance drawn from an inverse chi-squared distribution. When σ2\sigma^2σ2 is unknown and drawn from a broad distribution, the marginal distribution of xxx has heavier tails than any single Gaussian — the extra uncertainty about the scale inflates the probability of large deviations. As ν→∞\nu \to \inftyν→∞, the Student’s t converges to a Gaussian.

Source
torch.manual_seed(305)

μ0   = torch.tensor(0.)
κ0   = torch.tensor(1.0)
ν0   = torch.tensor(2.0)
σ2_0 = torch.tensor(2.0)

# Analytical marginal p(μ) = St(ν0, μ0, σ2_0/κ0)
t_dist = StudentT(ν0, μ0, torch.sqrt(σ2_0 / κ0))

# Empirical samples from the NIX prior
σ2_samp = ScaledInvChiSq(ν0, σ2_0).sample(sample_shape=(10000,))
μ_samp  = Normal(μ0, torch.sqrt(σ2_samp / κ0)).sample()

μ_grid = torch.linspace(-6, 6, 200)
bins   = torch.linspace(-6, 6, 31)

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.hist(μ_samp.numpy(), bins.numpy(), density=True,
        alpha=0.4, color='gray', ec='k', label='NIX samples')
ax.plot(μ_grid, torch.exp(t_dist.log_prob(μ_grid)),
        '-r', lw=2, label=r"$\mathrm{St}(\nu_0, \mu_0, \sigma_0^2/\kappa_0)$")
ax.plot(μ_grid,
        torch.exp(Normal(μ0, torch.sqrt(σ2_0 / κ0)).log_prob(μ_grid)),
        '--', color='purple', lw=2,
        label=r"$\mathcal{N}(\mu_0, \sigma_0^2/\kappa_0)$")
ax.legend(fontsize=11)
ax.set_xlim(-6, 6)
ax.set_xlabel(r"$\mu$")
ax.set_ylabel(r"$p(\mu)$")
ax.set_title("Student's t as the marginal of the NIX distribution")
plt.tight_layout()

Posterior Credible Intervals¶

One of the main uses of the posterior is to construct credible intervals — Bayesian analogs of confidence intervals that have a direct probabilistic interpretation: Iα\mathcal{I}_\alphaIα​ is a 1−α1-\alpha1−α credible interval if Pr⁡(μ∈Iα∣X)=1−α\Pr(\mu \in \mathcal{I}_\alpha \mid \mbX) = 1 - \alphaPr(μ∈Iα​∣X)=1−α.

Under an uninformative prior, the posterior marginal is μ∣X∼St(N,μML,σML2/N)\mu \mid \mbX \sim \mathrm{St}(N, \mu_{\mathsf{ML}}, \sigma^2_{\mathsf{ML}} / N)μ∣X∼St(N,μML​,σML2​/N), and the central 1−α1-\alpha1−α credible interval is

Iα=[FSt−1 ⁣(α2 ∣ N,μML,σML2N),  FSt−1 ⁣(1−α2 ∣ N,μML,σML2N)],\begin{align} \mathcal{I}_\alpha = \left[F_{\mathrm{St}}^{-1}\!\left(\tfrac{\alpha}{2} \,\Big|\, N, \mu_{\mathsf{ML}}, \frac{\sigma^2_{\mathsf{ML}}}{N}\right),\; F_{\mathrm{St}}^{-1}\!\left(1 - \tfrac{\alpha}{2} \,\Big|\, N, \mu_{\mathsf{ML}}, \frac{\sigma^2_{\mathsf{ML}}}{N}\right)\right], \end{align}Iα​=[FSt−1​(2α​∣∣​N,μML​,NσML2​​),FSt−1​(1−2α​∣∣​N,μML​,NσML2​​)],​​
(24)

where FSt−1F_{\mathrm{St}}^{-1}FSt−1​ is the quantile function of the Student’s t distribution.

Equivalently, note that the standardized quantity

t=μ−μMLσML/N  ∣  X  ∼  St(N,0,1),\begin{align} t = \frac{\mu - \mu_{\mathsf{ML}}}{\sigma_{\mathsf{ML}} / \sqrt{N}} \;\Big|\; \mbX \;\sim\; \mathrm{St}(N, 0, 1), \end{align}t=σML​/N​μ−μML​​∣∣​X∼St(N,0,1),​​
(25)

so testing whether a hypothesized value μ∗\mu^*μ∗ lies in Iα\mathcal{I}_\alphaIα​ is equivalent to checking whether the test statistic t∗=(μ∗−μML)/(σML/N)t^* = (\mu^* - \mu_{\mathsf{ML}}) / (\sigma_{\mathsf{ML}} / \sqrt{N})t∗=(μ∗−μML​)/(σML​/N​) satisfies ∣t∗∣≤FSt−1(1−α/2∣N,0,1)|t^*| \leq F_{\mathrm{St}}^{-1}(1 - \alpha/2 \mid N, 0, 1)∣t∗∣≤FSt−1​(1−α/2∣N,0,1).

This is structurally identical to the frequentist one-sample t-test, but with a different interpretation: instead of asking “how often would this test reject under repeated sampling?”, we ask “given the data we observed, how probable is it that μ\muμ lies outside this interval?” The Bayesian and frequentist answers happen to coincide numerically here (under the uninformative prior), but they represent fundamentally different epistemic claims.

Conclusion¶

This chapter developed Bayesian inference for the scalar Gaussian distribution from the ground up. The key takeaways are:

  • Conjugate priors make posterior inference tractable: the prior and posterior belong to the same family, so updating reduces to a simple update of hyperparameters.

  • The normal–inverse-χ2\chi^2χ2 prior is the natural conjugate for a Gaussian model with unknown mean and variance. Its two shape parameters ν0\nu_0ν0​ and κ0\kappa_0κ0​ can be interpreted as pseudo-observations informing the variance and mean estimates respectively.

  • The Student’s ttt distribution emerges naturally as the marginal distribution of the mean after integrating out the unknown variance — heavier tails reflect our additional uncertainty about the scale.

  • Bayesian credible intervals have a direct probabilistic interpretation that differs conceptually from frequentist confidence intervals, even when the two coincide numerically under uninformative priors.

Next Steps

  • The Multivariate Normal Distribution — extends all of these results to vector-valued observations x∈RD\mbx \in \reals^Dx∈RD

  • Hierarchical Gaussian Models — builds multi-level structure on top of the conjugate Gaussian model

Recommended Reading

Bishop, 2006, Ch 2.3, 3.4.3. See also: Murphy, 2023, Ch 2.3.

References¶
  1. Bishop, C. M. (2006). Pattern recognition and machine learning. Springer.
  2. Murphy, K. P. (2023). Probabilistic Machine Learning: Advanced Topics. MIT Press. https://probml.github.io/pml-book/book2.html
Part I: Foundations
Bayesian Modeling
Part I: Foundations
